coredata.dc:title: Photo-anodes based on TiO2 and carbon dots for photo-electrocatalytical measurements
coredata.dc:creator: Aliyeva, Humay
Gurel, Ari
Nowak, Sophie
Lau, Stéphanie
Decorse, Philippe
Chaguetmi, Samiha
Schaming, Delphine
Ammar, Souad
coredata.dc:description: Wide band-gap semiconductor TiO2 is an efficient anode material for photo-electrocatalytical (PEC) applications. Its light-sensitivity is limited to the UV but can be extended to the visible by chemical doping or chromophore sensitization. Moreover, its conductivity can be enhanced by coupling with carbon materials, which should limit back electron-hole recombination. In this context, we prepared hetero-nanostructures based on TiO2 nanofibers supported on Ti sheet and impregnated with carbon dots (CDs), and used them as photo-anodes in photo-electrochemical cells. They showed a higher photocurrent than non-CD-impregnated photo-anodes, underlining the importance of CDs for water-splitting processing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanofibers
Carbon dots
Photo-electrocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image and (B) UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs; (C) XRD pattern, (D) Raman spectrum and (E) SEM image of TiO2/Ti.
(A) Raman spectrum of CDs-TiO2/Ti sheet; (B) Ti2p, (C) C1s and (D) O1s XPS spectra of CDs, TiO2/Ti and CDs-TiO2/Ti.
Photocurrent responses of TiO2/Ti and CDs-TiO2/Ti using several electrolytes and bias potentials.

head.ce:title: Photo-anodes based on TiO2 and carbon dots for photo-electrocatalytical measurements

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université Paris Diderot, Sorbonne Paris Cité, ITODYS, CNRS-UMR 7086, 75013 Paris, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université du 20 Août 1955 de Skikda, Faculté des Sciences, 21000 Skikda, Algeria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wide band-gap semiconductor TiO2 is an efficient anode material for photo-electrocatalytical (PEC) applications. Its light-sensitivity is limited to the UV but can be extended to the visible by chemical doping or chromophore sensitization. Moreover, its conductivity can be enhanced by coupling with carbon materials, which should limit back electron-hole recombination. In this context, we prepared hetero-nanostructures based on TiO2 nanofibers supported on Ti sheet and impregnated with carbon dots (CDs), and used them as photo-anodes in photo-electrochemical cells. They showed a higher photocurrent than non-CD-impregnated photo-anodes, underlining the importance of CDs for water-splitting processing.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen production by solar light-driven water splitting is a promising technology. However, it requires photo-anodes which convert light energy into chemical energy efficiently. Several semiconducting nanomaterials have already been used as photo-electrodes. In particular, TiO2 shows promise [1], due to its good electrical and light-dependent properties, its chemical stability, its low cost and its environmental safety. Nevertheless, work on improving its performance is in progress, principally by extending its activity to the visible range or by decreasing back electron-hole recombination. Thus, TiO2 has been modified by doping or functionalization [2,3]. For example, carbon materials such as nanotubes [4], graphene [5–7], fullerene [8], etc. have been used to increase the photocatalytic properties of TiO2, because of their high electron mobility and their ability to promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, enhancing charge-transfer processes [9–11]. Carbon dots (CDs) constitute a new class of carbon nanomaterials with no equivalent in other families of carbon-based nanomaterials [12–15]. They have the same fascinating physical properties as quantum-dots, but do not contain heavy metals, which makes them safer and more environmentally friendly. Their semiconductor-like electronic structure allows them to absorb visible light while their graphene-like crystallographic structure improves interfacial charge mobility [16] and separation [17]. Thus, CDs combine several important properties needed to enhance semiconducting properties in photo-assisted electrocatalysis applications.

Some studies have recently shown the capabilities of CDs to enhance photocatalytic properties of TiO2 [18,19]. In this letter, we investigate the effect of impregnation of TiO2 nanofibers (NFs) by CDs on their photo-electrochemical (PEC) response, when immersed as anode materials in a standard PEC cell, by comparing their photocurrent to that of bare TiO2 NFs. An important increase of the photocurrent has been observed in the presence of CDs, showing the capability of CDs to enhance photo-electrocatalytic properties of semi-conductors such as TiO2.

TiO2/Ti electrodes were obtained in treating Ti sheets in a 5 wt% aqueous oxalic acid solution at 100 °C for 2 h, and then immersing them for 24 h in a solution of H2O2 (30 wt%) and NaOH (10 M) in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 80 °C. CDs were synthesized using an aqueous solution (40 mL) of citric acid (1.68 g) and urea (1.44 g) in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 160 °C for 4 h. CDs-TiO2/Ti nanocomposites were prepared by impregnation of TiO2/Ti sheets in a solution of CDs after acidification with HNO3.

High-resolution transmission-electron microscopy (HRTEM) and scanning-electron microscopy (SEM) were performed using microscopes operating at 200 and 5 kV, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed in grazing conditions using a Cu-Kα X-ray source (1.5418 Å). Raman spectra were recorded using a laser at 633 nm. High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (HR-XPS) was carried out with a monochromatic A1-Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV), with a spot size of 500 µm (15 kV, 150 W); spectra were acquired in the constant-analyzer energy mode with a pass energy of 40 eV.
The photo-responses of the two photo-anodes, TiO2/Ti and CDs-TiO2/Ti, were evaluated in a three-electrode PEC cell, under intermittent xenon lamp (150 W) illumination.
On HRTEM micrographs, the CDs appear as 3–5 nm-sized spherical particles (Fig. 1 A), with a well-resolved lattice spacing of 2.1 Å, matching the (1 0 0) crystal planes of graphite [20].

UV–visible absorption spectroscopy performed on a colloidal aqueous solution of CDs (Fig. 1B) indicates that CDs absorb principally in the UV range, with a shoulder in the visible range around 550 nm.

The XRD pattern of TiO2/Ti (Fig. 1C) exhibits well-defined peaks matching perfectly the tetragonal TiO2 anatase structure (ICDD no. 00-021-1272). Additionally, its Raman spectrum (Fig. 1D) shows modes at 399, 517 and 638 cm−1 corresponding to the B1g, A1g + B1g and Eg vibrations, respectively, of the anatase phase [21]. Finally, SEM observation (Fig. 1E) confirms TiO2 appears as entangled fibers less than 100 nm in diameter and several µm in length.

The surface of the TiO2/Ti sheet, initially white, becomes blue after impregnation by CDs, suggesting the presence of CDs, agglomerated on the surface of TiO2 NFs and/or within their inter-porosity. The Raman spectrum of the resulting hetero-nanostructure (Fig. 2 A) exhibits clearly the signature of both anatase and graphene-type phases. Indeed, a total of five bands were observed, three at 399, 517 and 638 cm−1, assigned to crystalline TiO2, and two at 1350 and 1550 cm−1, assigned to CDs. The band at 1550 cm−1, usually called the crystalline G-band, corresponds to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon, and that at 1350 cm−1, called the disordered D-band, is related to the presence of sp3 carbon defects [20,22].

The surface chemical compositions of TiO2/Ti and CDs-TiO2/Ti were also investigated. Ti2p HR-XPS spectra of the two samples exhibit the same signature: two Ti signals at 459.5 and 465.2 eV, assigned to classical binding energies of titanium in TiO2 and corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 Ti(IV), respectively (Fig. 2B). The C1s HR-XPS spectrum of TiO2/Ti (Fig. 2C) is consistent with previously reported XPS data for TiO2 materials [17]. Three peaks with binding energies of 285.6, 287.5 and 289.6 eV, mainly attributed to the adsorption of organic residues, are observed. The first peak corresponds to C–C and C–H bonds. Those at higher energies correspond to C–O and CO bonds from carbonates, leading to Ti–C–O and Ti–O–C bond [23,24]. The C1s HR-XPS spectrum of CDs consists of a strong peak at 284.8 eV, usually attributed to sp2 carbon atoms [20], which is broadened and followed at higher energies by a small, broader peak assigned to C–O and CO bonds, present at the surface of CDs. The C1s of CDs-TiO2/Ti seems to be a combination of those of TiO2/Ti and of free CDs. At low binding energies, the graphitic sp2 (at 284.8 eV) and the aliphatic sp3 (at 285.6 eV) signals are observed, related to the impregnated CDs and organic residues adsorbed at the surface of TiO2 NFs, respectively. At higher energies, the intensity of the carbonate peaks is enhanced, due to the presence of CDs adsorbed at the surface of TiO2. The O1s spectra of these samples confirm the former conclusions, since we can clearly distinguish the signature of both CDs and TiO2 on the CDs-TiO2/Ti spectrum (Fig. 2D). Indeed, oxygen atoms from carbonates at the surface of CDs exhibit a typical signal at 532.0 eV, which is present in both free CDs and CDs-TiO2/Ti spectra. The oxygen atoms of the anatase lattice exhibit also a specific signature at 531.0 eV, which is present in the spectra of TiO2/Ti and CDs-TiO2/Ti. The peak at 530.3 eV observed in the CD spectrum corresponds to ITO, which has been used as substrate to deposit CDs for the XPS analysis.

As a consequence, Raman spectroscopy and XPS measurements confirm the presence of CDs at the surface of TiO2 nanofibers. As a negative zeta potential has been measured for CDs, they should be linked at the surface of TiO2, which is positively charged after soaking in the acid bath, by electrostatic interactions.

PEC properties of TiO2/Ti and CDs-TiO2/Ti were investigated by measuring the photocurrent produced during intermittent illumination, at different bias potentials. Firstly, we used an electrolyte of sulfites (Na2SO3) and sulfides (Na2S) (Fig. 3 A, B). This mixed electrolyte has already been found suitable for hydrogen production, because sulfides can be oxidized to disulfides at the anode and then be regenerated in solution by the reaction of the disulfides with sulfites [25]. With this electrolyte, at zero bias, the photocurrent density reached 90 µA/cm2 on TiO2/Ti, and increased to 160 µA/cm2 at a 500 mV/SCE bias. Replacing TiO2/Ti by CDs-TiO2/Ti led to an approximately three-fold increase in the photocurrent density: 270 and 390 µA/cm2 at 0 and 500 mV/SCE, respectively.

Such a synergetic effect of CDs with TiO2 can be particularly valuable for hydrogen generation. In order to carry out water-splitting, we performed photocurrent measurements in Na2SO4, a non-electroactive electrolyte (Fig. 3C, D). As expected, the photocurrent measured was lower than in the previous experiment. Under these conditions, the photocurrent density reached 5 and 10 µA/cm2 at 0 mV/SCE and increased to 8 and 21 µA/cm2 at 500 mV/SCE, on TiO2/Ti and CDs-TiO2/Ti, respectively. This decrease in the photocurrent compared to the experiments performed with the previous electrolyte is due to the absence of electroactive species. As a consequence, the photocurrent measured is the result of the oxidation of water at the TiO2/Ti or CDs-TiO2/Ti photo-anodes (and, consequently, the reduction of protons at the platinum counter-electrode).

One can assume that CDs contribute in the generation of photo-electrons and that they promote interfacial charge-carrier separation. Charge transfer at the photo-anodes must be accompanied by water oxidation at the surface of both nanostructures. The enhancement of the photocurrent using CDs can be explained not only by a photosensitizing effect but also by a decrease in electron-hole recombination inside the TiO2. Characterization of the CD-TiO2/Ti electrodes has been again performed after photo-electrochemical assays, and no significant change has been observed, demonstrating the stability of the TiO2-based photo-anodes as already observed otherwise [26].

In summary, we have described the preparation of CDs-TiO2/Ti hetero-nanostructures based on the formation of TiO2 nanofibers by the corrosion of Ti sheets and their subsequent impregnation by CDs. The PEC properties of the resulting electrode were measured, and a strong enhancement of the photocurrent was observed, compared to bare TiO2/Ti. This enhancement is attributed to the photosensitizing ability of the CDs and their interfacial charge-carrier separation contribution. These results are promising for hydrogen generation.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests.
